transcript:
Here's a mesh of glowing nodes strung together with red lines, starting out cool and scattered — and within about ten seconds, that whole field bleeds into warm orange, just a few green stragglers holding out at the edges.

The thing to catch is what's actually driving that color: near the top, there's a variable called correction history, and every time two particles get yanked back into their constraint by the physics solver, it climbs — capped at 1.0, because "even we have limits," as the comment says. That number is literally piped into the color math a few lines above render: teal at zero, gold at one. So the warming you're watching isn't decoration, it's a running tally of how many times each particle has been corrected, painted directly onto the particle.

And notice the two colors aren't named "cold" and "warm" — they're named resistant self and compliant residue. The piece isn't showing motion settling down. It's showing a self getting overwritten by the record of its own corrections, one constraint-solve at a time.
